title: StringBuilder
description: The mutable alternative to String, solving the loop-concatenation performance problem from the Immutability lesson.
objectives: Understand why StringBuilder exists and how it differs from String
Use append(), insert(), delete(), reverse(), and toString()
Recognize when StringBuilder is the right tool vs plain String

sections.text: In Strings and Immutability, we saw that result = result + i; inside a loop creates a brand new String every single iteration, since String can never be modified in place. StringBuilder exists specifically to solve this.
A mutable sequence of characters that can be modified in place — appended to, inserted into, or deleted from — without creating a new object each time, unlike String.

Unlike String concatenation, sb.append(i) modifies sb IN PLACE — no new object is created on each call. This is the entire performance benefit: for a loop running thousands of times, StringBuilder does far less unnecessary work than repeated String concatenation.
3. toString() — Converting Back to a String

StringBuilder is not a String — it's a different type entirely. Once you're done building, .toString() converts it into an actual, immutable String, which is what you'd typically return from a method or store long-term.

sections.output:
Hello, World
Hello World
dlroW olleH

sections.title: Common StringBuilder methods
rows: Adds x to the end, modifying the StringBuilder in place
Inserts x starting at the given index
Removes characters from start (inclusive) to end (exclusive) — same range rule as substring
Reverses the entire sequence of characters, in place
Converts the StringBuilder's current content into a String

sections.text: Notice System.out.println(sb); worked directly, without calling .toString() explicitly — println automatically calls toString() on whatever object you give it. We call it explicitly here for clarity, but it isn't strictly required just to print.
5. When to Use StringBuilder vs String

sections.title: Guideline
points: Use plain String for a value that's built once and doesn't change — most everyday text handling
Use StringBuilder when building up text incrementally, especially inside a loop — one append per iteration instead of one new String per iteration
Convert back to String with .toString() once you're done building, if you need to pass the result somewhere expecting a String

sections.text: new StringBuilder(text).reverse().toString() chains three steps in one line: wrap text in a StringBuilder, reverse it in place, then convert the result back to a String for the .equals() comparison. This chaining pattern — calling one method directly on the result of another — is common with StringBuilder.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using String concatenation (+) inside a loop that runs many times, instead of StringBuilder's append() — a common, easy-to-miss performance issue.
Forgetting StringBuilder is a different type than String — you cannot pass a StringBuilder directly where a String is strictly required without calling .toString() first.
Assuming delete(start, end) and substring have different range rules — both are start-inclusive, end-exclusive, consistent with the earlier String Methods lesson.
Reversing a StringBuilder and forgetting it modifies the object in place — sb.reverse(); already changed sb itself, calling it again would reverse it right back.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Why does StringBuilder exist when String already covers text?
questions.answer: Because String is immutable, repeated modification (like concatenation in a loop) creates a new object every time, which is wasteful for many operations. StringBuilder is mutable, so operations like append() modify it in place without creating new objects each time.

questions.question: How do you convert a StringBuilder back into a String?
questions.answer: Call its toString() method, which returns an actual, immutable String representing the StringBuilder's current content.

questions.question: When would you choose StringBuilder over plain String concatenation?
questions.answer: When building up text incrementally, especially inside a loop with many iterations — StringBuilder avoids creating a new object on every single append, which repeated String concatenation would otherwise do.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: StringBuilder is a mutable sequence of characters, created to solve the performance problem of String's immutability: repeated concatenation with + creates a new String object every time, which is wasteful in a loop with many iterations. StringBuilder's append(), insert(), delete(), and reverse() all modify the same object in place. Once built, .toString() converts it back into an actual String. The general guideline: use plain String for text built once, and StringBuilder when building text incrementally, especially inside loops.

sections.title: Trick to Remember
sections.text: String = a printed page (Immutability lesson). StringBuilder = a whiteboard — erase, append, insert, reverse, all on the SAME object. Call .toString() when you're ready to print the final page.

sections.title: Quick Understanding Check
questions: Why is StringBuilder more efficient than String concatenation inside a loop?
How do you convert a StringBuilder into a String?
Does append() create a new object each time it's called?
What does delete(start, end) remove — is end inclusive or exclusive?
